title: How Single Moms Can Rebuild Confidence and Find Balance After Burnout
guestBio: Jessica Renee is a solo mama, part-time hairstylist, Arbonne rep, and wellness advocate who is on a journey of rebuilding confidence and balance after experiencing burnout in the early years of motherhood. She's passionate about helping other moms reclaim their identity while balancing entrepreneurship and self-care.
excerpt: Jessica Renee shares her raw, honest journey from losing herself in early motherhood to intentionally rebuilding her confidence and purpose. This conversation is a masterclass in starting over, one small step at a time.
metaDescription: Discover how single mom Jessica Renee rebuilt confidence after burnout, balanced entrepreneurship with motherhood, and reclaimed her identity.
keyTakeaways: It's never too late to start rebuilding your life and confidence, even after experiencing complete burnout.
Single motherhood doesn't disqualify you from pursuing entrepreneurial dreams and personal growth.
Fueling yourself first isn't selfish—it's essential for showing up as your best self for your family.
Small, intentional daily habits can create massive shifts in confidence and overall life balance.
Using tools like AI can help streamline both work and home life, giving you precious time back.
Rebuilding your identity after motherhood requires patience and intentional work, but it's absolutely possible.
Wellness habits and mindset shifts are foundational to creating lasting change in your life.

pullQuotes.text: You can rebuild, one small step at a time—it's never too late to start again.
Fueling yourself first changes everything in how you show up for your family.

timestamps.label: Jessica's journey from burnout to rebuilding
Using AI tools for life optimization

content:
There's something profoundly powerful about watching a woman rebuild her life from the ground up. In this episode, Aimee sits down with Jessica Renee, a solo mama who embodies the truth that it's never too late to start again. Jessica's story isn't just inspiring—it's a roadmap for any woman who has ever felt like she lost herself in the beautiful chaos of motherhood and wonders if there's a way back to who she was meant to be.

As a part-time hairstylist, Arbonne rep, and wellness advocate, Jessica is living proof that you can pursue multiple passions while raising children on your own. But her journey wasn't always this clear. Like so many of us, Jessica found herself completely burned out in the early years of motherhood, feeling like she had disappeared into the role of just mom. The woman she used to be felt like a distant memory, and the dreams she once held seemed impossible to reach.

The Reality of Losing Yourself in Motherhood

Jessica's honesty about losing herself resonates with countless mothers who have experienced the same identity crisis. When you become a mom, especially as a single mother, the weight of responsibility can feel overwhelming. Every decision, every moment, every ounce of energy gets poured into caring for your children. While this devotion is beautiful, it often comes at the cost of your own sense of self.

What makes Jessica's story so compelling is her recognition that this loss of identity wasn't permanent—it was simply a chapter in her story, not the ending. She made the conscious decision to begin the slow, intentional work of finding herself again. This wasn't about abandoning her role as a mother or becoming selfish with her time. Instead, it was about understanding that she could be an incredible mom and a woman with her own dreams, goals, and identity.

Currently reading The Manage Her, Jessica has found validation in the concepts of invisible labor and leadership at home. These themes speak directly to her experience as a working mom and entrepreneur who is learning to value her own time and energy as precious resources that deserve protection and intentional use.

Rebuilding Confidence One Step at a Time

The journey from burnout to balance doesn't happen overnight, and Jessica is refreshingly honest about this reality. Rebuilding confidence after feeling completely depleted requires patience, self-compassion, and a willingness to start small. Jessica has discovered that confidence isn't something you wait to feel—it's something you build through consistent action.

Her approach to confidence building is beautifully practical. Rather than waiting for some magical moment when she would suddenly feel ready or worthy, Jessica began taking small steps toward her goals. Each action, no matter how tiny, became evidence that she was capable of more than just surviving—she was capable of thriving.

This mindset shift is crucial for any woman who has ever felt like she's starting from zero. You don't need to have it all figured out to begin. You don't need to feel confident to take confident action. Jessica's journey shows us that confidence is built in the doing, not in the waiting.

The Revolutionary Act of Fueling Yourself First

One of the most powerful themes in Jessica's story is her understanding that fueling herself first isn't selfish—it's strategic. As mothers, we're often conditioned to believe that putting ourselves first is wrong or indulgent. We're taught to pour from an empty cup until we're completely depleted, and then somehow find more to give.

Jessica has learned that this approach is not only unsustainable but also unfair to the people we love most. When you fuel yourself first—through wellness habits, mindset work, pursuing your goals, and honoring your own needs—you show up as a better version of yourself for everyone in your life. Your children benefit from having a mother who models self-care and personal growth. Your business benefits from having a leader who operates from a place of abundance rather than depletion.

This isn't about becoming selfish or neglecting your responsibilities. It's about understanding that you are worthy of the same care and attention you so freely give to others. Jessica's wellness advocacy stems from this core belief that taking care of yourself is taking care of your family.

Entrepreneurship as a Path to Freedom

Jessica's journey as an entrepreneur—balancing her work as a hairstylist with her role as an Arbonne rep—demonstrates that single mothers can absolutely build businesses while raising children. The key is finding ways to work smarter, not just harder. This is where Jessica's use of technology, including AI tools like ChatGPT, becomes particularly interesting.

Rather than trying to do everything manually, Jessica has embraced tools that can streamline both her work and home life. This approach gives her precious time back—time she can spend with her children, invest in her personal growth, or dedicate to building her businesses. It's a perfect example of how modern mothers can leverage available resources to create more space in their lives.

Her entrepreneurial journey isn't just about making money—it's about creating options and building a life that aligns with her values and priorities. Every business decision is filtered through the lens of whether it supports the balanced, intentional life she's working to create.

Permission to Start Again

Perhaps the most important message in Jessica's story is the permission it gives other women to start again, regardless of their circumstances or how far off track they may feel. Whether you're a single mother, married, divorced, starting a business, changing careers, or simply trying to rediscover who you are outside of your roles and responsibilities, Jessica's journey is proof that it's possible.

You don't need anyone's permission to pursue your dreams, but sometimes we need to hear from someone who has walked a similar path that it can be done. Jessica provides that voice of encouragement and practical wisdom. She shows us that rebuilding your life doesn't require dramatic, sweeping changes—it requires consistent, intentional choices that align with the woman you're becoming.

Her story is particularly powerful for women who feel like they've waited too long or missed their chance. Jessica is living proof that there is no expiration date on your dreams, no age limit on personal growth, and no circumstance that can permanently disqualify you from creating a life you love.

If you're navigating burnout, questioning your identity, or wondering if it's possible to rebuild your life while managing the demands of motherhood, Jessica's conversation with Aimee is exactly what you need to hear. It's a reminder that you have everything within you to create the change you desire—you just need to take the first small step.

guestLinks.label: 📚 Grab your copy of The Manage Her

quiz.title: What's Your Rebuilding Style?
quiz.description: Every woman's journey to rediscovering herself looks different. Take this quiz to uncover your unique approach to rebuilding confidence and finding balance.

questions.question: When you think about making changes in your life, what feels most true for you?

options.text: I prefer to start with small, manageable steps that I can build on over time
I'm ready to make bold moves and take on new challenges head-first
I want to find the most efficient path forward using the right tools and systems
I need to work on my mindset and inner world before I can make external changes

questions.question: When you're feeling overwhelmed or burned out, your first instinct is to:

options.text: Take a step back and focus on one small thing I can do today to feel better
Look for a bigger solution or opportunity that could change everything
Analyze what's not working and research better ways to manage my time and energy
Examine my feelings and figure out what my burnout is trying to tell me

questions.question: How do you view the relationship between taking care of yourself and taking care of others?

options.text: I'm learning to balance both by making small daily choices that honor my needs too
I believe showing my family what's possible requires me to go after my own dreams
The more efficiently I can manage my own life, the more present I can be for others
I can't pour from an empty cup—my emotional wellbeing affects everyone around me

questions.question: What does 'finding yourself again' after a major life transition look like to you?

options.text: Slowly reconnecting with old interests and discovering new parts of myself
Pursuing goals and dreams that I may have put on hold
Creating systems and routines that support who I want to become
Understanding how I've changed and what I truly value now

questions.question: When it comes to building confidence, you believe:

options.text: Confidence grows through consistent small wins and self-compassion
Confidence comes from pushing yourself outside your comfort zone
Confidence builds when you have the right strategies and feel prepared
Confidence starts with truly knowing and accepting yourself

questions.question: If you could change one thing about your current life situation, you'd focus on:

options.text: Creating more moments of peace and intention in my daily routine
Opening up new possibilities for growth and achievement
Making everything run more smoothly so I have more time for what matters
Feeling more aligned with who I really am at my core

results.title: The Gentle Rebuilder
results.description: You believe in taking small, thoughtful steps toward change. You understand that lasting transformation happens slowly, and you're willing to be patient with yourself as you rediscover who you are beyond your roles and responsibilities. You value sustainable progress over dramatic shifts. This episode will resonate deeply with you because Jessica's story shows how small, intentional daily habits can create the massive shifts you're seeking without overwhelming your already full life.

results.title: The Bold Reinventor
results.description: You're ready to make significant changes and aren't afraid to shake things up in pursuit of your dreams. You see challenges as opportunities and believe that taking calculated risks is worth it when it comes to rebuilding your life. You're energized by the possibility of transformation. Jessica's entrepreneurial journey in this episode will inspire you, showing how single motherhood doesn't have to limit your biggest ambitions and how you can pursue multiple passions simultaneously.

results.title: The Practical Optimizer
results.description: You approach rebuilding by looking for smart systems and efficient solutions. You want to maximize your time and energy, and you're always seeking tools and strategies that can help streamline your life. You believe in working smarter, not harder. This episode is perfect for you because Jessica shares practical insights about using tools like AI and creating wellness habits that actually stick, giving you actionable ways to reclaim precious time in your day.

results.title: The Heart-Centered Rebuilder
results.description: You prioritize emotional healing and mindset work as the foundation for any life changes. You understand that true transformation starts from within, and you're committed to doing the deeper work of understanding yourself and your patterns. You value authenticity and connection above all else. Jessica's honest discussion about losing and finding her identity after motherhood will speak directly to your soul, validating your experience and showing you that prioritizing your own wellness isn't selfish—it's essential.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hey everyone, welcome back to The Manager, the podcast where we celebrate the unseen work of women and turn it into a blueprint for leadership, healing, and self-discovery. I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author, entrepreneur, mama of six, and your weekly reminder that you're allowed to lead, build, and rise on your own terms.

Today's guest is Jessica Renee, a powerhouse solo mama, serial entrepreneur, and wellness advocate who's redefining what it means to not get lost in motherhood. Jessica is a former permanent makeup artist turned part-time hair stylist, a self-made businesswoman, and an Arbon rep, helping women feel better, look better, and live better. She's built her business around self-care, confidence, and community, and she's a beautiful example of what it looks like to show up for your own dreams. She's also currently reading The Manager, and today we're going to talk about how the themes in the book are showing up in her life.

From the shift in her identity to her new rhythm as a working mom building something she loves. In this episode, we will talk about single motherhood and rediscovering self-worth, creating balance after burnout, and how to dress better, feel better, and live better from the inside out. Let's get into it.

Hey, Jessica. Let's start with hello.

**Jessica:** Hi.

**Aimee:** How are you?

**Jessica:** So good. How are you?

**Aimee:** Good. I'm so glad to have you here today.

**Jessica:** Thank you. Your place is beautiful. It feels good to be here.

**Aimee:** Oh, yay. Welcome to The Manager. So, when was that moment that you realized you were losing yourself in motherhood? That turning point.

**Jessica:** I feel like I was on a hamster wheel. It was Groundhog's Day. It was just the same thing over and over and over again. And I was going to work, I was coming home, cooking dinner, putting my daughter to bed, waking up, doing breakfast, going to work, and I was just on and on and on. And my fiance at the time was going to the beach with my daughter and just had this work life balance, probably because I was handling most of the bills, but I had this work life balance. And I was like, what? Wait a minute. What am I doing? I need to break away from this a little bit. Reanalyze what's important and stop living the same life every single day.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Get off the hamster wheel. Yeah. So, you're just super burnt out. There's no way to live like that. You weren't putting your oxygen mask on at all. You had no time for yourself.

**Jessica:** No. No time for myself. Yeah. I was living off of coffee and wine and just so caught up in building my career and trying to provide and trying to live this big life, but I was not pouring into myself at all. I felt drained. I felt emotionally disconnected. 

I was feeding Jazelle one time in her high chair and nothing bad, but I just was feeding her and I'm like, I feel nothing right now. And it scared the heck out of me because I was just on this rotation.

**Aimee:** I think a lot of moms get to that place, especially when their kids are little and you start to feel like a mother zombie. You get to that place where you're like, I am numb. There's nothing left. So, what was the turning point for you when you were like, let's put the brakes on it.

**Jessica:** Yeah. Well, that scared the heck out of me when I was feeding her. And then I used to get gnarly panic attacks. So, I kind of circled back into that of like, oh my gosh, what's going on? I'm burning myself out. I'm not happy. Not that I didn't love my fiance and my daughter, but there was just something missing. 

And I was going to work and I worked with amazing women, but they were also doing the same thing. It was like go to work, overwork ourselves, not eat, not healthy, go to the bars, drink at night, be hung over in the morning, take your clients, work through it. And I'm like, there has to be more. I know that there's people that live bigger lives, better lives, working less, not trading time for money. So, how the heck do I get there? So, then I just kind of went on this eye opening seeking like where is it? Where is it? Looking for it. And then I finally found it.

**Aimee:** How did you find it?

**Jessica:** So, I started doing personal styling. I'm like, I'm going to cut back my hours at the salon and do personal styling because that's what I love too is clothes. But I realized on that journey that it was also just as much trading time for money. But one of my clients who I styled invited me to a networking event which was actually an Arbon event. And so I went there and I'm like, "Oh, beautiful girls. They are in front of people what a great networking opportunity for personal styling and hair."

And when I met them, they didn't push me for the business. They were just like, "Come as you are. We welcome you." I went to dinner with them. Just had a really good night. And they were talking about all the fun things that they do because they work from their phone. And so I was driving to work the next day and I'm like, "God, my cup feels so filled after just being around positive people who are traveling and talking about big ideas and not just what they did last night and what they're going to do tonight, but big ideas."

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Jessica:** And it was almost like God slapped me in the face. It was like, "Hello, this could be an opportunity." And so I just ran with that. And I'm like, I'm always the one that's like, "Okay, I'll try it. Okay, I'll try it." And when you join a company that is so deep in personal development, it really opened your eyes and really helps you grow. So that was kind of like my intro to the path of personal development and just taking a step back and reanalyzing your life.

**Aimee:** Amazing. Amazing. And now you've got a little more balance in your life. So now you're doing hair three days a week and then running your other business on the side and you're able to bring in the same amount of money, more money. What's it like for you now?

**Jessica:** Yeah, it's amazing to not have to rely and be so scared like, "Oh my gosh, I need more clients." Summer's coming. During summer, during January, and then April, clients dip because it's just certain times of the year. So before I'd be like, shoot, scraping for money. Because with hair, you can feel like you're rich and you're like, oh my god, I'm so abundant. And then all the clients are on vacation and you're like, I have nothing.

So, this really helped me fill my schedule full for three days and then have the other days to tend to my daughter, to tend to my needs, to think about like, wow, I have the whole day off. What do I want to do? What's important to me? Maybe it's going for a walk. Maybe it's meeting friends. But the craziest part has been like, I have 4 days to live my life. And sometimes I'll be I just went to lunch with a friend and I'm in Newport Beach looking at the ocean eating. I was like, wait, should I be doing something else right now? It's like, "No, just enjoy."

**Aimee:** Yeah. So, all of a sudden, you're finding yourself and you're a free person. You're not on a hamster wheel anymore.

**Jessica:** Exactly.

**Aimee:** So, you found you had to think your way out of it.

**Jessica:** Yes. And really live in each moment of like, "This is what I wanted to do. This feels good." Going to the gym, being like, "No, I am taking time to do this." And reprioritizing what's important, right?

**Aimee:** Yeah. And putting yourself maybe at the top of your priority list.

**Jessica:** Oh, yeah. Absolutely. Because you can't give to others if you're completely depleted, right? So, it has to be you and then if you're in a marriage, then your marriage, your children, and then your work, right?

**Aimee:** Yeah. Turning it all on its head, putting this back on the top because honestly, if mommy doesn't put on her oxygen mask, right?

**Jessica:** Oh, yeah. You have to. You have to.

**Aimee:** And we can get stuck in Orange County especially, I'm sure other metropolitan areas, but get stuck. Work is so important. Work, work, work. But it's like you can work to be happy to make time to be happy. Work isn't your happiness unless I mean I love what I do, you love what you do, but we still need to take time to do other things so that we can be abundant with each other and...

**Jessica:** Absolutely. And not everybody loves what they're doing for work. Unfortunately.

**Aimee:** Unfortunately. I'd love to see a world where women are doing the things they love to do.

**Jessica:** Oh yeah. Because then there's magic everywhere.

**Aimee:** Yes. Everywhere. Yeah. That is my bigger dream is if women could all get out of the grind and be able to do maybe it's a little bit of hair, maybe it's a little bit of this and then a little bit of that, and it's all of a sudden finding the rhythm, a new rhythm in your life where you have a little more time for yourself, a little more time for the things that are your priorities like your kids, your health, your exercising, what you eat, because honestly to eat good food even takes time.

**Jessica:** It does. It absolutely does. And then to actually when you're eating the meal to enjoy it and be like, "Look at this beautiful meal I'm eating. This is delicious." Because it actually nourishes your body more when you're eating it and enjoying it instead of just scarfing something down to move on.

**Aimee:** Totally. You have to slow down.

**Jessica:** That's the hardest thing for me still. If I have time to just sit down and enjoy what I'm eating, even if it's once a day, I'm thrilled.

**Aimee:** Yes. Yeah. And it's hard because I feel like you're a multitasker.

**Jessica:** I'm a multitasker and we're eating. We could be doing so many other things, but I think my biggest thing for this year is to just slow down and everything is intentional. Like even eating a meal.

**Aimee:** Nice. I love that. I love that. I hope every woman kind of sets an intention for the year. That's another really good personal trick to do. Mine this year was connecting with my community. I was like, Aimee, you need to build the community around you. You need to spend more time with these women. And that's how everything came about with the podcast.

**Jessica:** It's powerful, especially after we don't want to mention 2020, but so many people got reclusive. And so then to create community again, we all need it. We all need people by our side. We all need smiling faces, resources, it's so important.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Because if we're communicating, even if you're just communicating a goal or a vision or a manifestation for yourself and you're around people, they'll help you to do that. If they know what you're trying to do, your tribe will lift you up.

**Jessica:** Absolutely. Yeah. And sometimes it's not about who you know, but it's about who they know. And you can only do that through community and meeting new people.

**Aimee:** Exactly. It's such an important thing that we are getting back to now. And I'm so glad we're getting back to it because women especially, mothers especially cannot do this alone. We cannot. It's just not a happy place for us. It's just not something that human female mothers were meant to do this parenting thing all alone.

**Jessica:** 100%.

**Aimee:** Yeah. And the more community you have around you as a mother, the happier you're going to be. And your kiddos, too.

**Jessica:** Yeah. Absolutely. Because then they'll have the friends. you have the friends and you have the support because sometimes you're just like, "Hey, my kids eating paper. Is this normal?" They're like, "Oh, yeah. Mine too. Don't worry. It's just a phase. You're good." And it's like I just feel like sometimes you just having those especially when you're in the motherhood. Having a community around you can just save your sanity.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. And even just to be able to joke about things like my kid is eating paper and they're like, "Yeah, yeah, don't worry. They all do it." They're like, "Okay, crazy kids," and just make things light-hearted instead of like, is something wrong? Is something wrong? What's wrong all the time? "Why are they doing that?" It's like, "Nah, they're kids. Let them eat dirt. Let them eat paper."

**Jessica:** Totally. Exactly. And it's so I mean, it's very much the tribal way that we raise children in circle with other women and other children. And there wasn't this aloneness that we've created such an isolated culture and then COVID really brought out the worst of the isolation culture and shined a big old spotlight on isolation culture and people got stuck in it.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah. And bust me out. I would live on a commune honestly, bunch of other moms and families. We'd all be together for lunch.

**Jessica:** Exactly. I know. And I feel like there's a lot of single moms that feel that way. I would always wonder is it ever going to happen, right? Like who's going to do it? Who's going to do this? Because I remember when one of my yoga teachers had her daughter, she was like, Aimee, let's just go raise our kids. Let's get some other single moms and we'll do this.

**Aimee:** That's so funny. Big land and just lean on each other.

**Jessica:** Yeah. Yeah. There was a moment between my marriages where I was like, hmm...

**Aimee:** I know. Then I kind of like having a man, too.

**Jessica:** Yeah. I do. I do. I guess I'm a little traditional that way. So, you talk a lot about confidence. What are some tangible ways that moms can bring their confidence back?

**Aimee:** Great. Yes. One is definitely taking time for yourself. I love what Mel Robbins does, the high five method. So, looking at yourself in the mirror and just giving yourself a high five. For me, looking at yourself in the mirror in the beginning sometimes can be challenging because you're like, I have so many other things to do. Why am I sitting here? But if you look at yourself in the mirror, say you have a vanity or you're in your bathroom, the lights are on, you're staring at yourself, you're doing your skincare routine, then say nice things about yourself like, "Wow, your eyebrows look so good today." Or like, "Look at my lashes are so long." Or putting your makeup on and being like, "Wow, my cheekbones look so great." Or, "My skin is so shiny today." But just start complimenting yourself and taking that sacred time to be with you and saying nice things about you.

**Aimee:** I love that so much because there's so much against that in our culture and it's actually in the book I have the first exercise is the love letter to you because it's like if we can't practice self-love it's really hard to do anything else if you can't if you don't love you like it's very hard to find success in life.

**Jessica:** Oh yeah because then other people if you don't love yourself you don't leave space for other people to love you. And I think that we're so in an era where it's like Botox and filler and this and that. And I mean I promote beauty products. But so many people are focused on tearing themselves down. Like oh my dark circles under my eyes, my lines on my face. I'm not tan enough. Whatever it may be. So and then what you speak about comes about. So if you really just take time to say lovely things to yourself and you can even bring your children into it if you're like I don't have a moment alone. Bring your kid in. You say one thing you love, your kids says one thing that they love about themselves.

**Aimee:** I love that. We talked about that and it was like I have to make sure we talk about this because so many moms say, "I don't have time to put on my makeup or put on a cute outfit. My kids won't let me." And you have the answer.

**Jessica:** Yeah. Your kids can be an excuse or it could be the reason. So I turn it into my reason and now it's so fun. She's four, but we did it when she was two. When she was two, she can't dress herself. So, for the younger kids, I would be like, "Okay, mommy's going to do her makeup" and give her some coconut oil so she could put on her face while I did my makeup and my skincare. And then I'd give her a clean brush that was just hers so she could pretend to put her makeup on. 

And then we would go to the closet and I'd be like, "Should I wear this shirt or this shirt?" And then she would help me pick out my outfits. Say it takes an extra 10 minutes to get ready. Just plan that out. Wake up 10 minutes earlier. But then you're living in each moment too with your kid instead of like you go play this or daddy take care of her so I can hurry up and get ready. It's like then you're enjoying getting ready again. You're enjoying taking care of yourself.

**Aimee:** Yeah. And you're teaching your kid about taking time for herself and how to take pride in her appearance.

**Jessica:** Yeah. It's important. It is important. And it's not saying that you have to be egotistical about how you look or wear the best outfits or buy the most expensive stuff. It's just about loving on yourself. And maybe you just do a little mascara and lip gloss or maybe you like to do a full face. It's whatever makes you feel beautiful is what you should do. And wear outfits that make you feel beautiful because then that light shines out to everybody. And when you're shining and you're radiating love and happiness, that's what you receive.

**Aimee:** Oh, yeah. Absolutely. I just love that so much because I remember, oh my gosh, there was a time after I had my first child that I was such a hot mess and I had no idea. I had a partner who had the house above us who was my business partner and his little girl came down with a hairbrush one day and was like, "Aimee, can I brush your hair?" Julian was probably about 6 weeks old and I was like, "I haven't slept. I haven't showered. I'm just full on the definition of the new mom." He rocked my world so hard. But you can get so lost in it. Like oh my gosh, I can't even take a shower. You've got a newborn baby. You're freaking out. And it's like finding community, finding time for yourself. Remember that it is important to take care of yourself. Like I see you, mama. I see you. Take care of yourself. Go brush your hair.

**Jessica:** Yeah, go brush your hair. You have to put on some lipstick and mascara. You'll feel way better. Yeah. Get out of the house, go take a walk, put the baby in the stroller, go for a walk.

**Aimee:** Yeah. There's just postpartum depression can be real and the hormones that come along with it. When you're in that, it's quite a long window of postpartum. It can be four or five years. It's intense. So, asking for help, too. Like tell your person what you need.

**Jessica:** Yeah. And then also too, which is really nice, is if you go out on a walk sometimes you can feel so stuck or you're like I don't know how to talk to other people even though you crave that and you need that and you just want to be seen. So what I think is really nice is when someone walks by just compliment something. I love your shoes. Or oh your shirt's really nice or have a great day just nice things like that where you're talking to others because when you're giving a compliment or saying something positive it's going to come back at you and that's going to start building up your confidence and your love box.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Exactly. And maybe you'll spark a conversation, maybe you'll make a new friend. And that's what you really need when you're a new mom. It's all about making mom friends at that time because if you don't already have friends with kids, you're going to be in the isolation bubble again. And it's like go join a class for little kids. Go get in music class. Go meet some mommy friends. Like you have to or you're going to be all alone and sad. And nope, you don't need to be sad. Go get happy, go make some friends.

**Jessica:** Yeah. Get outside. It's so easy to fall into it. And you wrote, complacency is death. People are afraid to leave good for great. Can you unpack that for us?

**Aimee:** Yes. I feel like so many of us when you really ask how do you like your job? Are you happy? Yeah. Everything's good. Everything's good. But if you really think is it good? Are you really happy? If you keep doing this until you're 70 and then you look back, would you be like, I lived a really good life or did you just get by?

And sometimes you need a little shake up because it's crazy. Our minds will actually choose a familiar hell over an unfamiliar heaven.
